speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4 of an interview with Alberta Szalita. Today is December 11th, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we left-- we left Warsaw on September 6, Wednesday. The train-- when we got into the train, there was no space to sit down. I was sitting on, on the floor of the entrance. And the train didn't leave till the next morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Went near-- near [INAUDIBLE], that-- outside of Warsaw, the train was bombed. And we run out of the train. And next to the train, there was growing cabbage. And in-between the cabbage, there, we were lying there. And, and they, they were shooting over our heads, the, the Germans. I didn't watch. I thought they maybe might have fallen asleep. And my husband was watching the, the flights. And then they-- they stopped the bombing. We were not hurt. And we went straight into the forest, those who were on the train. A lot of people, military people, in this-- in, in the forest didn't know what-- waited. I thought maybe the officers know how-- where you should stand, or what you should stand. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but of course, we were frightened by this whole experience. And I, I was trusting that the men know better what-- how to hide, where to stand, how to protect oneself. And they were all very distraught. And some of them plainly had diarrhea. For me, this-- that day was a kind of a crisis because I always assumed girls are afraid. I saw myself as a frightened little girl. You-- you've got a different picture of me. But I thought I was afraid. And to make a long story short, I couldn't eat the whole 24 hours. And then the next day-- so, yeah. We, we waited until this commotion was calmed down. And we started to walk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But, but the next day, we stopped in a village. And in the village, the-- they-- somebody said that there, there is a doctor with-- I was a doctor. And we had-- in the rucksack, we had some kind of first aid things with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we couldn't get in touch with anything. And you couldn't call up because the post office didn't work. We could not get in touch with our parents. We wanted to get in touch to let them know that we are on the way there or something. We couldn't get in touch with them. Nothing worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the fact that I was a doctor-- so somebody needed a doctor-- that then they gave us food. And this is how we spent three weeks. We walked. We walked at night. And in daytime, we were hiding in a-- somewhere in the-- in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] barns in the village. And in every village where we stopped, the fact that I was a doctor was-- said to people that there is a doctor so that they know to use me. And, and gave us-- they gave us to eat. It means mostly there-- that were eggs and ham, fried eggs, ham, and bread. And we washed in the river or something like this and walked three weeks. On the 27th of September, we arrived to Luck. Well, I could tell stories about the, the trip. But let us go on because it's not-- we're not going to finish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we arrived to Luck on the 27th. And the first man that we met in the street was a, a Polish officer, doctor. It had-- he had-- wear red here, velvet on the-- anyway, a doctor that I knew very well that was-- Kleinbaum was his name, Moshe Kleinbaum. He-- he subsequently was in Israel and his name was Sneh. The people-- S-N-E-H, Sneh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we went to my, my in-laws. You-- myself, [? Zonya ?] and myself, we stayed with the in-laws-- went to home to my parents and found everybody in good shape. And started the Soviet with-- on the way-- on the way as we passed [? Bug ?], we learned that Ribbentrop and Molotov made an agreement to divide Poland. And we found ourselves in the Russian part of it. And, well, we saw people with red something around their arms. And so there we are. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People are alive. They prefer what is-- the war-- the war doesn't exist anymore because this part is the Russian protection. It was the Russians that liberated us, supposedly. And that started the time of the Soviet Union. My father-in-law hated the, the Reds, just plain hated. He was a Russian, Russian, Russian-born and a Russian officer in the Czar's Army. So he was not happy about the whole thing. I cannot say whether I was happy or unhappy. The fact is, there we were. We were out of war. We are not in any danger. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Since-- the things, we left in Warsaw. And on the way-- because I couldn't carry much, I'd throw things out and throw things out just to-- less to carry. And we walked nights. Sometimes [? Zonya ?] was keeping my-- my rucksack. And we were walking. I was almost walking asleep. And in daytime, it was like sport. It wasn't bad. We managed. And the fact that we managed gave me some satisfaction that I was useful. And I came, came there. I don't know yet what to do. Yeah, I remember I was in my sister's [? house ?]-- learned-- and she graduated Polish literature. But she also learned to sew. So she was showing me how to, to sew myself pajamas, and so on. So that's what I did for-- and then I read. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pretty soon, of course, there was health services, Russian Health Services. I was invited to, to get-- to-- to the health department. And I was given a job. I was given a job to be the head of maternal and child care for that section of Volyn, one of the sections. About a million population that we had to organize, the Russian Health Services.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came home. I told my father-in-law that that's the job they gave me. So he got very angry. What do you know about administration? I mean, he was angry at them, angry at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I know nothing. Naturally, I don't know. I never had an administrative position. He was the director of the hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, again, to make a long story short, pretty soon he was asking my advice what to do there with the Russian administration. Because I learned fast whatever there was to learn about the administrative aspect of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pretty soon, I had four jobs, combined one with another. It's called [NON-ENGLISH]. It means you connect the jobs. First, I had this [NON-ENGLISH], which is called [NON-ENGLISH]. 'Nomenklatura,' it means an important position, the head of this maternal child care. Then they, they wanted me to train the nurses [NON-ENGLISH]. That means without taking them away from work to learn to be nurses, army nurses. That was another job. The third, they had to have a committee to decide who needs to get a pension and what kind of a pension, limited pension, or more. So they needed a committee, which was called [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], That's a-- which I was a neurologist. Because I was a neurologist. And the fourth job is at [NON-ENGLISH]. The welfare department was organizing a home for physically handicapped children. They wanted to demonstrate these free health services. I was very enthusiastic about that job. Because at the time when I was in this hospital for children in Warsaw when I was learning to be a physician, a pediatric-- a pediatrician for children, the-- we brought them-- a woman brought-- a Jewish woman brought a child very sick with pneumonia. He needed hospitalization. And she had to-- she needed-- the child needed the hospital. But, in order to leave the child in the hospital, she had to pay for a month stay. And she didn't have money. And I, I was on duty at that time. So I decided that she needed hospital. But the rest was not in my hands. And, and I didn't have money to pay for her because I would have. And they sent her away home. And she was raising hell. She was screaming, and there was a policeman standing. And the policeman asked her for-- anyway, she had to take this child home. And I never forgot that situation. So here, they have free health for everybody, without payments. So naturally that was very appealing to me. So I was-- I was satisfied. My husband got a job-- not-- not-- not necessarily a job that he would have liked because there was no industry in Luck, but a job that he could work. My father got a job. My sisters got jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for us-- for us, it was, at that time, a kind of stable-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and taking in account-- it's not that I was happy with what happened to Poland. I was really unhappy about this whole thing that they decided to divide the, the country, and so on. It was against my grain.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at any rate, as far as existence, it was a kind of a stable situation. And I, I was kind of contented. And we lived-- the reason we lived with my in-laws, me and [? Zonya ?], was because they had the big apartment. And the-- if, if we wouldn't stay there, the Russians would put in other people there, because they were not-- they were bringing a lot of people. And they needed-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they needed space. And they did-- and they did terrible things, as a matter of fact. We had-- in the same house at my-- in the-- at my in-laws, one room was occupied by a lawyer who came from-- was a refugee. And the, the Russians did announce that whoever, whoever wants to go back home should sign up that they wants to go back home, meaning going back to Warsaw. And if they don't want to, they had to say whether they want to stay or they want to go back. And whoever wrote that, they is going-- wants to go back, instead of sending them back, they took them to Siberia. They started the Soviet kind of, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, behavior. Some of my uncles, my mother's brothers, had to leave Luck because they, they, they moved them out. They were not entitled to stay in the city. So that it started-- for some people, it started, the misery, right from the start, but not, not, not for us. My, my father-in-law hated them. But he worked as a physician. And he had this position. He did not suffer any physical inconvenience. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, I could go on describing what the Soviets do--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father-- situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father got a job. The only person who did not-- who did not look for a job was my mother. But, I mean, as I told you, I got four, four, four [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pensions that they paid me, I mean, four salaries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one salary, I gave my mother. I was very happy to bring her the salary from one of the salaries so that she should feel also that she's having an income. So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so all was well, so to speak. And all was not well.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, but so you, you can see that it-- so sometimes for one person, it's, it's good. For another person, it's not so good, or it's bad. That man that lived in our-- I was telling him-- lived with us. I was telling him, don't sign that you want to go back-- don't sign that you want to go back to, to Poland because I didn't trust them. They already knew-- from, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from my office, I already knew. But he-- now, he wasn't going to sign that he's not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he's not going to go back because otherwise, he has to accept Russian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] citizenship. Well, anyway, he was shipped off. Maybe, maybe it was even good that he was shipped off because maybe he was-- he was alive. Because if he would have gone back to Warsaw, then he would have probably had perished. Well, whatever, that's a-- they did not tell the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just send him off. And a lot of people were sent off like that from, from Luck. Luck had a lot of refugees. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, again, I'm going to jump ahead. It came 1941, and I wanted to see Russia inside. Because whomever they brought, sent people-- supposedly communists and members of the Communist Party-- were ignoramuses. It was really something that's unbelievable to compare-- to compare that the Soviet [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] intellectuals, or whatever-- supposedly. I could tell stories, a lot of stories, about it how I, I recognized that the inadequacy of, of these people-- so just plain inadequates, ignoramuses. And I don't exaggerate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, anyway, so it came '41. And I said, I, I wanted to see Russia inside, how it is. Is it different or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that-- some of those that they send from Russia to, to instruct us were very well-- very well-educated people. Those that, that they send to work there were on a very low level. But some of those that came from Kiev to, to give us some instruction were very educated people. Well, I am easy on, on, on-- to go. I like to travel, just as I said at the time when I wanted to be a physician. And at that same more or less period, I said, I live in the world. I want to see it. So I wanted to see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm going to go for five days. I had an opportunity because the head of the health service in, in Luck said, there is a conference going on-- to go in Odessa. So if you want to go, you can go. So she started to tell me, why are you going? There's going to be a war again. Because we had television-- not television. We had a radio, Telefunken-- which you could listen to BBC every morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I heard the BBC in English what's going on. And they were saying that the Germans are going to attack Russia. Well, so this-- we knew that this war was going to take place. So I said, well, I'm going to come back in five days. It's only five days that I'm going. Well, so this was June 14. On the 15, I would've gotten my salaries. But on the 14, they didn't pay yet. I'll tell you why I'm mentioning now the money because that will be clear in a second. Well, at any rate, I got the permit. I got the [NON-ENGLISH], which means you get-- you have to get from the police a, a, a permit-- a permit. I was just reading them through this morning again. And I went, I got myself an outfit for-- it was June 14-- nice, elegant dresses, and so on, summer things. And, and I went. At [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my in-laws, my mother-in-law's sister came from Kiev to visit her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And her daughter that-- that sister's daughter wanted to buy a new coat. And she wanted my, my in-laws to lend her money to buy a coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I talked them in into lending the money. And I said, well, I'm going to go to this conference. So get this money back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I went to there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We flew two of us-- one friend of mine who recently died, also a refugee from Warsaw-- flew to Kiev, and from Kiev by train to Odessa. And Odessa was a very-- an interesting conference, lack of vitamins in the food. But the issue was not for me the conference, nor the vitamins, or whatever, but just to see-- to see a little bit of the Soviet Union. And there in the hotel, I met Norwegians who were going to join the English Army through Russia. So we're dancing there in the evening. It was all just festive. On the 20th-- so a couple of days later, we're going back-- going back to Kiev, going to be at that cousin's house. And from Kiev, we will fly to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- back to Luck. That day, I couldn't get a good place, you know, in the train, the first-class train. I wanted [NON-ENGLISH], as they call it, a soft seat. So I left a day later. And I arrived Saturday to Kiev. And Saturday in the middle of the night-- yeah, on Saturday, he-- that cousin, my husband's cousin, [? Kreucheck ?], he was a professor at the university. So they-- I was invited them for-- to dinner with them. We went to the dinner. And in the middle of the night, I hear anti-aircraft shooting. Anti-aircraft shooting I knew from Warsaw, the, the sound of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we woke up.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why anti-aircraft shooting? He said, well, you're talking nonsense. What, what would the anti-aircraft be shooting in the middle of the night now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so in the morning, we already knew that Kiev was attacked and was bombed. Kiev, the same--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the same night. And I couldn't already, already go back. I couldn't get a plane to go back, nothing. And that's it. Now, I have to go to the bathroom. So you have to interrupt the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you have to stop for a minute. I should--
